metadata.title: Why Two Checkpoints Fail When One Virus Infects
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = intact / functional / normal cell circuit; CRIMSON = disabled / lost / virus-hijacked
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no HPV type taxonomy beyond naming 16 and 18; no CIN grading system detail; no other viral carcinogens (EBV, KSHV); no HPV vaccine mechanism; no integration vs episomal HPV distinction. All illustrative numbers labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.title: Title card
beats.narration_text: A virus too small to see does in one infection what normally takes two separate mutations accumulating over years in the same cell.

viz.headline: Why Two Checkpoints Fail When One Virus Infects

beats.title: Cervical cell under HPV assault

props.headline: HPV-16 infects a cervical epithelial cell. Within hours, two viral proteins…

beats.narration_text: HPV-16 infects a cervical epithelial cell. Within hours, two viral proteins begin their work — quietly, with no DNA damage anyone can measure.

viz.description: Microscopy-style cross-section of a cervical epithelial cell being contacted by small virus particles. Cool clinical newsprint treatment.

beats.title: The question — one infection, two losses
beats.narration_text: Losing p53 and Rb together should require two independent somatic mutations — a rare coincidence. Here a single virus infection achieves both losses simultaneously. Why does one infection do the work of two mutations?

viz.question: Why does one infection do the work of two mutations?

beats.title: The two-hit expectation — normal mutation logic
beats.narration_text: Normally, tumor suppressors fall one at a time. A somatic mutation disables one copy, then another mutation hits the second. Two independent events — both rare, both required.

viz.description: Two independent mutation arrows, each striking one gene, shown sequentially over a timeline. CRIMSON arrows. TEAL = intact gene, CRIMSON = mutated.

beats.title: p53 — the damage guardian
beats.narration_text: p53 is the genome's guardian. DNA damage accumulates, p53 rises, the cell cycle halts, and repair begins. No p53 — no halt, no repair.

viz.description: Single-arm circuit: DNA damage signal -> p53 node -> halt signal. All TEAL, functional.

beats.title: Rb — the gate on DNA replication
beats.narration_text: Rb sits at the gate to S phase. It holds E2F — a transcription factor — sequestered. When a cyclin signal arrives, Rb releases E2F and the cell copies its DNA. Rb controls entry.

viz.description: Rb box holding E2F. Cyclin signal -> Rb releases E2F -> S-phase genes on. All TEAL, functional.

beats.title: Section card — how HPV does it
beats.narration_text: Here is how one virus dismantles both.

beats.title: E6 hijacks ubiquitin machinery to destroy p53
beats.narration_text: E6 is the first viral protein. It recruits E6AP — the cell's own ubiquitin ligase — and redirects it to tag p53 for destruction. The cell's own cleanup crew becomes the weapon.

viz.description: E6 (CRIMSON) grabs E6AP ubiquitin machinery, arrow to p53 node, p53 shrinks away and disappears.

beats.title: E7 binds Rb and forces E2F free
beats.narration_text: E7 is the second viral protein. It binds Rb directly and forces the Rb-E2F complex apart. E2F is released — not by a controlled cyclin signal, but by a viral crowbar. S-phase genes switch on regardless of conditions.

viz.description: E7 (CRIMSON) wedges between Rb and E2F, Rb shown grayed/disabled, E2F escaping upward toward S-phase genes.

beats.title: Both circuits dark — the split
beats.narration_text: Both circuits, simultaneously disabled. One infection event. No mutation in TP53, no mutation in RB1 — but both proteins gone. The DNA-repair gate is dark. The S-phase gate is open.

viz.description: Two parallel arms shown side by side. Upper arm: E6 -> p53 destroyed (CRIMSON). Lower arm: E7 -> Rb disabled, E2F free (CRIMSON). Both converge on a single node: checkpoint-free cell.

beats.title: Not mutation — protein-level hijacking
beats.narration_text: This is why sequencing finds nothing. E6 and E7 never alter a single DNA letter. The genes are intact. The proteins are gone. Sequence-level precision oncology would call this cell normal.

viz.description: Two columns: left = DNA sequence (TEAL, intact TP53/RB1 shown as clean bands), right = protein level (CRIMSON crossed-out p53, crossed-out Rb). Contrast the two layers.

beats.title: Permissive conditions for further mutation
beats.narration_text: And the cell is now permissive for further damage. No p53 to arrest it, no Rb to slow it — each division risks another mutation. Over years, that accumulation drives progression toward invasion.

viz.description: A simple damage-accumulation timeline: small marks accumulate on a bar representing the cell's genome over time, unchecked, each division adding more.

beats.title: Example — UV damage in a normal vs HPV-infected cell
beats.narration_text: Imagine UV light hits two cervical cells. In the normal cell, p53 rises within minutes, the cycle halts, repair begins. In the HPV-infected cell — same UV, same damage — no p53 response, no halt. E6 cleared it already. E7 freed E2F already. The DNA damage passes directly into S phase, unrestricted. Illustrative.

viz.description: Left cell (TEAL): UV arrow -> p53 accumulates (TEAL bar rising) -> HALT. Right cell (CRIMSON): UV arrow -> no p53 (flat, crossed) -> S phase proceeds, damage carried forward.

beats.title: Endcard
beats.narration_text: One infection. Two tumor suppressors neutralized. Not by mutation — by viral proteins that commandeer the cell's own machinery. That is why a virus does in minutes what somatic evolution takes years to accomplish.

viz.question: Why does one infection do the work of two mutations?
viz.answer: E6 and E7 hijack the cell's own machinery to eliminate p53 and Rb without touching a single DNA letter.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Biology series.
